title: Jonathan Tjaarda Kellogg
text:
About
I am a graduate student in Politics at the University of Oxford, transitioning to a DPhil at Balliol College under the supervision of Ben Ansell and David Rueda. My research focuses on comparative political economy, with a secondary interest in research methodology, particularly behavioural and experimental methods.
I am interested in how political institutions shape economic outcomes. My current projects combine survey experiments and large-scale text analysis to study how voters and legislators behave under information frictions.
Before Oxford I read Economics (MSc, Behavioural Economics and Game Theory) at the University of Amsterdam and Liberal Arts and Sciences (BSc, Governance, Economics and Development) at Leiden University College The Hague.

title: Research

title: Research
text:
Working papers

Frankenstein’s Bills: The Political Utility of Disjointed Legislation
MPhil thesis, University of Oxford, Trinity 2026
I treat disjointed legislation as a strategic tool: lawmakers bundle in secondary provisions the public is unlikely to notice. Using new measures of disjointedness for bills in France, the US, and the EU, I find that lobby spending predicts disjointedness, but only when an issue’s salience is low.
Paper (PDF) · Code


Tax, What Is It Good For? Perceived Government Efficiency and Redistribution Preferences
Working paper, 2026
Voters’ redistributive preferences may turn not just on fairness but on whether they think government spends competently. I use policy visibility as a proxy for perceived efficiency and exploit a regression-discontinuity around the UK Winter Fuel Payment: visible spending raises willingness to redistribute among British women, the gender asymmetry the theory predicts.
Paper (PDF) · Code

title: Pin the Headline
text:
A small game about reading political approval curves. You see a leader’s approval rating over time with the dates stripped, and a real headline from their tenure — can you pin it where it happened from the curve’s shape alone? Wars rally, scandals slide, market panics fall off a cliff.
Polling series are approximate reconstructions (Gallup, Ipsos MORI, YouGov, IFOP, Datafolha) and are for play, not citation.


Open the game full-screen →
Just here for the Americans? There’s a standalone US Presidents edition with every president of the polling era, FDR through Biden.
